Por favor, reformule esta información utilizando un enfoque diferente.

The H2-TPR profiles of the carbon-supported catalysts (Fig.1A) display two hydrogen consumption peaks: one sharper related to the reduction Ni nitrate precursor and other much broader in the range ~300-600 oC, attributed to the hydrogen consumption of residual Ni2+ or NiO particles along with the production of methane via reaction of hydrogen with the carbon support in the vicinity of Ni particles

Los perfiles de H2-TPR de los catalizadores soportados en carbono (Fig.1A) muestran dos picos de consumo de hidrógeno: uno más agudo relacionado con la reducción del precursor de nitrato de Ni y otro mucho más amplio en el rango de ~300-600 oC, atribuido al consumo de hidrógeno de las partículas residuales de Ni2+ o NiO junto con la producción de metano mediante la reacción del hidrógeno con el soporte de carbono en las proximidades de las partículas de Ni.
reescribeme el parrafo que te pasaré a continuación usando otras palabras
¡Por supuesto! Adelante con el párrafo que deseas que reformule.

It is interesting to note that, despite the very similar formed Ni particle size (as will be shown below), the modification of the HSAG support, either with oxygenated functional groups or with Nb2O5 particles, causes a slightly increase in the reduction temperature of the Ni nitrate precursor from 272 oC (Ni/HSAG) to 287 oC (Ni/HSAG-ox) and 292 oC (Ni/HSAG-Nb).

Es curioso destacar que, a pesar del tamaño de partícula de Ni formado muy similar (como se mostrará a continuación), la modificación del soporte HSAG, ya sea con grupos funcionales oxigenados o con partículas de Nb2O5, provoca un ligero aumento en la temperatura de reducción del precursor de nitrato de Ni de 272 oC (Ni/HSAG) a 287 oC (Ni/HSAG-ox) y 292 oC (Ni/HSAG-Nb).
en inglés

It is worth noting that, despite the very similar size of the formed Ni particles (as will be shown below), modifying the HSAG support, either with oxygenated functional groups or with Nb2O5 particles, causes a slight increase in the reduction temperature of the Ni nitrate precursor from 272°C (Ni/HSAG) to 287°C (Ni/HSAG-ox) and 292°C (Ni/HSAG-Nb).

As observed in the selectivity-time profiles depicted in Fig.7, both HHCPEN and HHD are detected during the course of the reaction, although with different relative proportion depending on the tested catalyst system.

As seen in the selectivity-time graphs shown in Figure 7, both HHCPEN and HHD are identified throughout the reaction, albeit in varying proportions depending on the specific catalyst system being tested.

Nonetheless, the presence of carbon deposits over the latter can not be discarded due to the observed shift in the maximum combustion temperature from ~500 oC (fresh Ni/HSAG-ox) to ~620 oC (spent Ni/HSAG-ox) of the carbon support, which points out to a lower gastification activity by Ni particles (Fig. S7), suggesting a partial deactivation of the latter.

However, the possibility of carbon deposits on the latter cannot be ruled out, as evidenced by the observed increase in the maximum combustion temperature from approximately 500°C (fresh Ni/HSAG-ox) to around 620°C (spent Ni/HSAG-ox) of the carbon support. This indicates a decrease in gasification activity by the Ni particles (Fig. S7), hinting at their partial deactivation.
a Ni catalyst with a selective hydrogenation ability for the HMF carbonyl group while retaining the furan ring
A nickel catalyst with the capability for selective hydrogenation of the HMF carbonyl group while preserving the furan ring.

However, poor hydrothermal stability along with too high density of acidic sites on the catalyst support (Ni/Al2O3) as well as sulfur impurities from the HMF feedstock are critical factors for catalyst deactivation by coke deposition and Ni poisoning, respectively.

Nevertheless, the catalyst's deactivation due to coke buildup and Ni poisoning is largely influenced by poor hydrothermal stability, excessively high density of acidic sites on the catalyst support (Ni/Al2O3), and sulfur impurities originating from the HMF feedstock.

Nevertheless, a poor hydrothermal stability and excessively high density of acidic sites on the catalyst support conducting to coke deposition (Ni/Al2O3) are factors that led to catalyst deactivation.

However, inadequate hydrothermal stability and an excessively high density of acidic sites on the catalyst support, leading to coke deposition (Ni/Al2O3), are factors that contribute to catalyst deactivation.

The results presented here will aid to better comprehend the complex chemistry of the aqueous-phase hydrogenation of HMF and can be valuable for developing highly selective Ni catalysts for the hydrogenation of biomass-derived HMF to valuable chemicals. Furthermore, we have shown that the presence of trace HMF impurities such as DMSO, frequently overlooked in the scientific literature, modifies the active sites by sulfur poisoning, which can lead to important erroneous conclusions on the catalyst’s performance.

The findings outlined in this study will enhance our understanding of the intricate chemistry involved in the aqueous-phase hydrogenation of HMF and can be instrumental in the development of highly selective Ni catalysts for converting biomass-derived HMF into valuable chemicals. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that the presence of trace impurities like DMSO in HMF, often neglected in scientific literature, alters the active sites through sulfur poisoning, potentially resulting in significant inaccuracies regarding the catalyst's performance.
